text: metallic sound of it was thunderous. Several clangs were followed by the noise of the object rolling on the concrete. He shifted his optics to follow the path in the dirt, ready to open fire. The can he’d kicked rocked slowly in place. There was more trash scattered around the floor — mostly cans and empty wrappers . Frowning, he moved forward, working his way through the maze of dangling fabric to the far wall. There were more boxes and plastic bins there, none in any better shape than what he’d seen upstairs. He turned to the left, through more sheets, and finally emerged in an open area . A workbench with a vise bolted near its center spanned the rear wall. More cans were scattered over its surface, along with small boxes, jars, and bottles. Articles of clothing, as dirty and worn as the bedding suspended from the rafters, hung along the edge of the bench as though placed there to dry . One item caught his attention, recognizable — despite the cobwebs enveloping it — because of the lever on one side. It was a press from a reloading kit . He shifted his optics to the floor, where a collection of blankets and pillows formed a pallet upon which three skeletons lay in ragged clothing. The two smaller ones were huddled in the arms of the third. Each skull had a small hole in its forehead . Several feet away, a fourth skeleton, taller than the rest, lay facedown on the concrete. The back of its skull was shattered, suggesting an exit wound . Ronin crouched beside the lone skeleton. Its fingers were wrapped around a rusted pistol, one arm twisted awkwardly beneath its torso. In its other hand, it held a paper wrapped in a plastic sheath. Ronin slid it out of the claw-like grip. The writing on the paper was in a sloppy scrawl, not unlike the latter entries in the journal Lara found in the attic . It was mercy. There was no other choice, and it was a mercy to do it. You’re not going to have us again, even after we’re dead. Not going to have us or ours! You made me kill them. Made me It was a mercy A MERCY Lord, have mercy on me . Folding the paper, Ronin stared at the remains. He’d spent many years longing for the return of his memories from the before time, had thought himself incomplete without them. But were these the sorts of memories stored in the corrupted part of his mind? Was the data full of scenes like this ? He lifted the hand and replaced the note. His optics flicked to the pallet; which of the smaller skeletons had been Lindsey ? Having lost his desire to search the basement further, he rose and walked to the workbench. The reloading press would likely trade for a good amount, but it would have to be enough regardless. He needed to get back to Lara . The bolts fastening the press to the benchtop were rusted, and groaned in protest when he clamped his pliers onto them and exerted pressure. For all that, they gave way easily. Within less than a minute, he had the press in his bag, sweeping in the nearby aluminum cans atop it . Lara would be pleased. He’d asked for a few days, but if he started back now, he’d be back in Cheyenne within twelve hours of leaving . He walked through the blankets, moving them aside with an outstretched arm. The air filled with displaced dust, obscuring his vision. Something caught at his ankle. It was thin, wire-like, and bent slightly. There was a short, metallic scrape, followed by the sound of another piece of metal falling onto the concrete . The fraction of a second Ronin had to anticipate what was coming proved too little. His processors fired off a volley of immediate commands . Leap away . Turn around . Shield optics and torso . Drop to the floor . The blast hit him before any of those actions were performed. His optics flickered to static, and his audio receptors took a burst of one hundred and eighty decibels before cutting out. His dermal sensors registered the impact first, a rush of hot air and then bits of shrapnel tearing his skin to ribbons. Sharp pain blazed through him. The sheets and blankets around him caught fire, falling atop him as he stumbled backward. His right leg locked, nearly toppling him over . He registered the intense heat both as a measurement of temperature and as searing agony that melted the electrodes in and beneath his skin . The haul . Whatever damage he had taken — was taking — they wouldn’t be prepared to leave Cheyenne if he didn’t bring in more salvage . Lifting both hands, he fumbled through the mess of burning fabric and melting synthetic flesh to tear the straps of his rucksack and thrust it away. His optics were chaotic, and he didn’t know if it was because of the fire or if they’d suffered more serious damage. Grasping whatever fabric he could, he pulled off the blanket and his burning coat, thrusting them away. He slid his foot back to put distance between himself and the blazing cloth . His heel caught on something — probably his discarded pack — and it was enough to disrupt his delicate balance. Ronin fell backwards. He was aware of the heavy thunk of his head hitting the concrete, and the majority of his systems went into standby . They had a trap set . You’re not going to have us, even after we’re dead . Diagnostics booted, assessing the damage . External temperature normalized. Casing penetrated in thirteen locations. Severe damage to synthetic epidermis, sixty-two percent loss. Power cells stable, 86.65 percent charge remaining . One by one, he powered his systems on . Motor functions impaired by damage to
